blurb: A bushy Sonoran-Desert penstemon hung with airy whorls of rose-pink to magenta trumpets over blue-green clasping leaves — a hummingbird magnet that blooms early and shrugs off heat, drought and lean ground.
water: Low; xeric once established — water monthly at most
seasons: Rose-pink trumpets spring into early summer; blue-green clasping foliage; tan seed stalks afterward
wildlife: Premier hummingbird plant; special value to native bees (Xerces)
origin: Sonoran Desert of the SW US (AZ, NM, CA) & NW Mexico
habitat: Desert washes, gravelly slopes & roadsides, 2,000–7,000 ft

care.hardiness: Reliably hardy to about USDA zone 5 (sources give zones 5–9/10), so it overwinters on the Front Range (Boulder ~5b–6a) in sharply drained ground; a Sonoran-Desert native, it is most at home in heat and lean soil and most at risk from wet winter feet.[2][3]
care.planting: Plant out in spring after the mid-May average last frost, or in early fall, into lean, gritty, fast-draining ground in full sun; do not amend with compost and skip mulch over the crown. In containers use a sharply draining cactus/gravel mix and keep the crown dry — potted desert penstemons rot far more easily than plants in open ground.[1][2]
care.sun: Full sun for best form and flower; it tolerates light afternoon shade in the hottest spots but gets leggy and shy-flowering in real shade.[2][3]
care.soil: Wants dry, well-drained, low-organic soil — gravelly or sandy is ideal — across a wide pH (about 6.1–8.5), so Boulder's alkaline ground suits it; it resents heavy, wet clay.[3]
care.water: Very low once established — it built deep taproots to ride out desert drought. Give spring moisture to bloom, then water monthly at most (accounting for rain); frequent or post-bloom overwatering causes crown rot.[2][3]
care.spacing: Space about 18–24 in apart; the bushy clump reaches roughly 2–3 ft tall in bloom and 1–2 ft wide.[1][2]
care.propagation: Grow from seed, which germinates best after cold-moist stratification (fall-sow outdoors, or chill the moist seed in the fridge before a late-winter start). Penstemons resent division — don't split the crown — so to clone a plant take basal or softwood cuttings instead; it also self-sows where happy.[1][3]
care.stratify: Cold-moist stratify the seed (fall sowing supplies the natural winter chilling) to break dormancy and germinate well.[1]
care.bloom: Airy whorls of rose-pink to magenta tubular flowers from early spring into early summer (March–June in its native range; later here), beloved of hummingbirds; deadhead spent spikes to neaten the plant and prolong bloom.[2][3]
care.feeding: No fertilizer needed in lean soil — feeding produces floppy growth and shortens its life.[1]
care.maintenance: Low: deadhead spent spikes, then back off water to prevent crown rot; tidy old flower stems in late fall or early spring and leave some seed to self-sow.[1][3]
care.troubles: No serious pests or diseases; root and crown rot strike in wet, poorly drained or over-amended soil, and rust, grasshoppers and flea beetles are occasional.[1]
care.companions: A hummingbird and native-bee plant for hot, dry borders, rock gardens and xeriscapes — combine with other desert penstemons, agastache, salvias and grasses in lean sunny ground.[2]

references.name: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center — Penstemon pseudospectabilis

edible.summary: Grown ornamentally for its hummingbird flowers — no documented edible use.[2]
edible.caution: No known culinary use; not documented as poisonous, but it is not a food plant — don't eat it.[2]
